prompt: Gradient descent rendered as a 1930s WPA Federal Art Project poster. A lone hiker (representing the optimization algorithm) descends a stylized mountain landscape (the loss surface), with a glowing red point marking the valley minimum. Flat areas of unmodulated colour — muted teal, cream, and brick red — no gradients or photographic shading. Bold blocky sans-serif lettering reading 'FINDING THE MINIMUM'. Strong silhouette, heroic upward-looking composition. Screen-print aesthetic with visible registration marks. No photorealism.
tradition_approach: Flat unmodulated colour areas, limited 3-colour palette, bold blocky sans-serif typography integrated into composition, strong heroic silhouette figures, screen-print aesthetic.
paradox: Gradient descent is an invisible, iterative mathematical process running inside a machine — yet WPA art was made to celebrate visible human labour and heroic physical effort. The algorithm becomes the worker.
visual_logic: The loss surface maps to a mountain landscape; the hiker's descent visualises iterative parameter updates; the red valley minimum marks convergence. Flat colour and silhouette enforce the tradition's graphic grammar throughout.
